text:
Continuing from where we left off, this entry provides an exegetical and theological analysis of the second part of the pericope dealing with the acquiring of wisdom, which is found in the Book of Sirach, (Sir 6:23–31). In the previous section (6:18–22), the importance of life-long learning for the individual was dealt with in great detail. The second section however, focuses on the stage in the life of the individual where he surrenders his life completely unto Wisdom, and what benefits or outcomes he can look forward to. The passage in the Book of Sirach (Sir 6:23–31), is structured in a concentric fashion. The outer parts (vv. 23–25 and 29–31) use the metaphor of slavery whilst the inner section (vv. 26–28) uses the imagery of hunting, as well as careful and diligent searching.

A considerable part of the chapter is devoted to a precise philological analysis of BS’s statement that one must abandon every type of personal freedom in order to follow the way of wisdom. The chains, collars and yokes represent initially the difficult austerity of the discipline to which the student must submit. However, the text includes a typical rhetorical periphrasis in which these instruments of constraint are suddenly transformed into “garments of honour”, necklaces of gold and crowns of joy. The author of the study therefore tries to demonstrate that for BS the way of wisdom represents a fundamental religious commandment, in that it follows on the invitation to “love God with all our strength and soul” (Deut 6.5), and that it is to this commandment that the student must submit, accepting that the weighty stone of the austerities of the school of the wise will be transformed into “rest and joy”, in the form of the social prestige that the crown of wisdom confers on those who obtain it.
